summary: Insurance companies use credit scores to set rates for homeowners and auto insurance, often charging people with poor credit double or more than those with good credit. O'Neil questions whether this practice reflects genuine risk assessment or exploitation of people with fewer options. She notes that homeowners insurance covers property contents, not mortgage payments, making the credit score connection dubious. The post also highlights that insurers target customers who don't shop around, undermining free market arguments. O'Neil frames this as predatory pricing that takes advantage of information asymmetry and limited choices for lower-income consumers.
key_points: Homeowners insurance rates can more than double based on credit score, despite covering property contents rather than mortgage payments
Auto insurance in Maryland charges people with bad credit 40% more, even though credit score doesn't indicate driving ability
Insurance companies deliberately target customers who don't comparison shop and charge them higher rates
The practice raises questions about whether pricing reflects actual risk or exploitation of people with fewer options
Free market competition fails when consumers lack good information
money_quotes: How much of this price discrimination comes from the insurance companies trying to understand and account for actual risk, and how much comes from their understanding that poorer people have fewer options and will simply pay predatory rates?
It's not a free market if not everyone actually has good information.
Someone with a poor credit score isn't obviously a bad driver.
Insurance companies actually target people who don't shop around and charge them more.
key_insight: Credit score-based insurance pricing may be less about measuring risk and more about exploiting the limited options of lower-income consumers.
